plainMeaning: I take up a never-ending wish to bow before Lord Śānti, who has stilled the world's great fears, who showers blessing like a rain-cloud, and who is an ocean of incomparable greatness.
recitation: śāntim upaśānta-bhava-bhūri-bhaya-paribhavaṁ, bhuvana-vana-sudhana-ghana-vāri-vara-vaibhavam | parama-śamam ihu sama-m-asama-mahimodadher natum īhām anantām ahaṁ sandadhe ||

wordByWord.gloss: Śānti (Śāntinātha, the 16th Tīrthaṅkara) — the accusative object of 'to bow'; the name simultaneously carries its plain sense 'peace'
wordByWord.etymology: from √śam 'to grow calm' + suffix -ti; the very name puns on the quietude (upaśama) the verse goes on to describe
wordByWord.notes: accusative singular; the punning of the name against upaśānta and parama-śama is deliberate yamaka

wordByWord.etymology: upa- (intensive) + śānta, past participle of √śam; heads the bahuvrīhi describing Śānti
wordByWord.notes: first member of the compound upaśānta-bhava-bhūri-bhaya-paribhavaṁ

wordByWord.gloss: worldly existence, the round of saṁsāra
wordByWord.etymology: from √bhū 'to become'; the cycle of becoming

wordByWord.notes: accusative singular closing the compound: 'by whom the world's abundant fear and humiliation is stilled'

wordByWord.etymology: from √bhū; 'that which has come into being'

wordByWord.notes: bhuvana-vana = 'the forest that is the world' — the parched thicket the rain-cloud nourishes

wordByWord.etymology: su- 'good, abundant' + dhana 'wealth, store'; here the cloud's rich store of water

wordByWord.notes: classical poetic word for the monsoon cloud; with vāri forms the rain-imagery
accusative singular; closes the second bahuvrīhi — Śānti 'whose splendour is that of a noble, water-rich rain-cloud over the world-forest'

wordByWord.etymology: from √śam; parama-śamam = 'supreme quietude itself', re-echoing the name Śānti
metrical/euphonic form of iha 'here' (with emphatic -u); preserved verbatim per the book's reading
wordByWord.notes: the book's spelling is honoured rather than normalized to 'iha'

wordByWord.etymology: a- (negation) + sama 'equal', joined to the preceding sama by a euphonic -m-; the chime sama-m-asama is pure yamaka
wordByWord.notes: the paradox 'equal-yet-unequalled' qualifies mahiman: equal to other Jinas in liberated nature, yet beyond compare in glory

wordByWord.etymology: mahiman 'greatness' + udadhi 'ocean' → mahima-udadhi, genitive singular; the visarga sandhis to -r before natum
wordByWord.notes: genitive governed by natum — 'to bow to [him,] the ocean of glory'
the act that the endless longing (īhā) is directed toward

wordByWord.etymology: from √īh 'to strive, desire'; feminine noun īhā, here accusative
wordByWord.notes: object of sandadhe; 'an unending longing to bow'

wordByWord.etymology: an- (negation) + anta 'end'; accusative feminine agreeing with īhām

wordByWord.gloss: I take up, fix firmly, set as my resolve
wordByWord.etymology: 1st person singular ātmanepada of sam + √dhā 'to put/join together'; here 'I conceive/undertake (a resolve)'
wordByWord.notes: the verse's main verb; the worshipper makes a standing, never-ending vow of homage

literalTranslation: Śānti — by whom the abundant fear and humiliation of worldly existence is wholly stilled; whose splendour is that of a noble, water-laden rain-cloud over the forest of the world; supreme tranquillity itself here; the ocean of a greatness at once equalled and unequalled — to bow to him, an endless longing I take up.
idiomaticTranslation: To bow before Śānti — he who has quelled the world's vast fears and indignities, who pours down blessing like a rich monsoon-cloud upon the forest of all beings, who is supreme peace itself, an ocean of glory both equalled and beyond compare — for this I conceive a longing that knows no end.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: Where the opening verse saluted Ajita ('naumi jinam ajitam'), this second verse turns wholly to Śānti, completing the individual homage before verse 3 weds the two as one Jina-pair. The poet builds the verse as a single sweep toward one verb, sandadhe ('I undertake'): everything before it qualifies the Lord, and the worshipper's act is reserved for the last breath of the stanza. Three portraits of Śānti are stacked. First, the moral: he is upaśānta-bhava-bhūri-bhaya-paribhava — one in whom the world's swarming fears and humiliations are extinguished; the name 'Śānti' is thus unpacked as actual peace, a pun sealed again by parama-śamam, 'supreme calm itself.' Second, the lyrical: bhuvana-vana-sudhana-ghana-vāri-vara-vaibhava paints him as a great monsoon-cloud whose noble waters revive the parched forest of the world — the compassionate teacher who cools beings scorched by saṁsāra. Third, the metaphysical: sama-m-asama-mahimodadhi, 'ocean of a greatness equal-yet-unequalled.' The chiming sama / m-asama is the verse's signature sound-play, and its paradox is doctrinal, not merely ornamental. The closing movement is the most tender: the worshipper does not merely bow, but 'takes up an endless longing (anantām īhām) to bow.' Devotion here is not a single act but a standing, inexhaustible orientation of the heart.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The 'equal-yet-unequalled' paradox (sama-m-asama) captures a precise Jain teaching: every liberated soul is identical in its intrinsic, fully-realized nature — so a Jina is sama, 'equal' — yet as a Tīrthaṅkara, a fordmaker who reveals the path, his glory is asama, 'without match' in the world of bound beings. The rain-cloud (ghana-vāri) is the standard figure for the Jina's compassion: like rain it falls without partiality, reviving the 'forest' of all life — anticipating the hymn's later image of teaching as nectar that cools souls burning in saṁsāra. Finally, fixing an 'endless longing to bow' (anantām īhām...sandadhe) frames namaskāra not as a momentary courtesy but as a sustained inner vow — bhakti as a permanent disposition of vinaya (reverent humility), itself a limb of right conduct.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: Reciting this verse, the devotee is invited to do two things: to take refuge in the one who has 'stilled abundant fear' — turning to Śānti precisely when the world's anxieties press — and to resolve, like the poet, not to a one-time bow but to an unending posture of reverence. The takeaway is that homage is a stance to be renewed, not a box to be ticked.

crossReferences.text: Logassa (Caturviṁśati-stava) — homage to the 24 Tīrthaṅkaras, of whom Śānti is the 16th
crossReferences.source: Panch Pratikraman Sutra — Vidhi Sahit (Anchalgachchha/Vidhipaksha ed.)

crossReferences.text: Laghu Ajitaśānti Stava (8th Smaraṇa, by Vīragaṇin) — the companion shorter hymn jointly praising Ajita and Śānti
crossReferences.source: Panch Pratikraman Sutra — Vidhi Sahit (Anchalgachchha/Vidhipaksha ed.)

sources.title: Monier-Williams Sanskrit–English Dictionary (1899), Cologne Digital Sanskrit Lexicon
sources.consultedFor: Glosses and roots for natum (√nam), sandadhe (sam-√dhā), ghana, vāri, vaibhava, śam/śānti.

sources.title: Wisdom Library — 'Iha, Īhā' (Sanskrit/Prakrit/Jain definitions)
sources.consultedFor: Confirming īhā as 'endeavour, longing, desire, striving' (√īh), distinct from the adverb iha 'here'.

sources.title: Wisdom Library — 'Paribhava, Paribhāva'
sources.consultedFor: Confirming paribhava as 'humiliation, insult, defeat, disregard' (pari-√bhū).

sources.title: Panch Pratikraman Sutra — Vidhi Sahit (Anchalgachchha / Vidhipaksha edition)
sources.consultedFor: Verbatim verse text and orthography (incl. the reading 'ihu'); placement of the Bṛhad Ajitaśānti as the 9th Smaraṇa.

notes: Token-level caveats: (1) the book reads 'ihu', a metrical/euphonic form of iha ('here') with emphatic -u, preserved verbatim rather than normalized. (2) 'masama' is a-sama 'unequalled' joined to the preceding 'sama' by a euphonic -m-; the deliberate chime sama-m-asama is yamaka and could alternatively be read as a single dvandva 'equal-and-unequalled' — the sense is the same paradox. (3) mahimodadheḥ is genitive (mahiman + udadhi) governed by natum, its visarga sandhied to -r before natum. (4) The main verb sandadhe (1st sg. ātmanepada, sam-√dhā) is read as 'I undertake/fix (a resolve)'; an alternate shade is 'I direct/apply (my longing)'. (5) Per the preface, authorship of this Bṛhad version is unattributed in the tradition source and is not guessed here.
